qa_pairs.question: Where can I find information about migrating to different standard environments?
qa_pairs.answer: For questions about migration to different standard environments, please see the [[Migration to the new standard environment]] page.

qa_pairs.question: What is the purpose of our standard software environments?
qa_pairs.answer: Our software environments allow you to switch between different versions of software packages, which are provided through a set of modules.

qa_pairs.question: How are the software modules organized?
qa_pairs.answer: The modules are organized in a tree structure. The trunk consists of typical utilities found in any Linux environment, branches represent compiler versions, and sub-branches are versions of MPI or CUDA.

qa_pairs.question: What specifically defines a standard software environment?
qa_pairs.answer: Standard environments identify common combinations of specific compiler and MPI modules used by our team to build other software. These combinations are grouped into modules named `StdEnv`.

qa_pairs.question: How many standard software environments were available as of February 2023?
qa_pairs.answer: As of February 2023, there were four standard software environments: versions 2023, 2020, 2018.3, and 2016.4.

qa_pairs.question: Which standard software environments are actively supported?
qa_pairs.answer: Only versions 2020 and 2023 are actively supported.

qa_pairs.question: What is the general rule for new software package installations?
qa_pairs.answer: In general, new versions of software packages will be installed with the newest software environment.

qa_pairs.question: Which standard software environment is the most recent iteration?
qa_pairs.answer: `StdEnv/2023` is the most recent iteration of our software environment.

qa_pairs.question: What are the default components of the `StdEnv/2023` environment?
qa_pairs.answer: The `StdEnv/2023` environment uses GCC 12.3.0, Intel 2023.1, and Open MPI 4.1.5 as its defaults.

qa_pairs.question: How do I activate the `StdEnv/2023` environment?
qa_pairs.answer: You can activate this environment by using the command `module load StdEnv/2023`.

qa_pairs.question: What is the minimum CPU instruction set supported by `StdEnv/2023`?
qa_pairs.answer: The minimum CPU instruction set supported by the `StdEnv/2023` environment is AVX2, or more generally, x86-64-v3.

qa_pairs.question: What is the default compiler in `StdEnv/2023`?
qa_pairs.answer: In `StdEnv/2023`, GCC becomes the default compiler, instead of Intel.

qa_pairs.question: How has the integration of CUDA and OpenMPI changed in `StdEnv/2023`?
qa_pairs.answer: In `StdEnv/2023`, CUDA is an add-on to OpenMPI. CUDA-aware MPI is loaded at run time if CUDA is loaded, which allows sharing of MPI libraries across CUDA and non-CUDA branches.

qa_pairs.question: Which core modules have been upgraded in `StdEnv/2023`?
qa_pairs.answer: The following core modules have seen their default versions upgraded in `StdEnv/2023`: GCC from 9.3 to 12.3, OpenMPI from 4.0.3 to 4.1.5, Intel compilers from 2020 to 2023, Intel MKL from 2020 to Flexiblas 3.3.1 (with MKL 2023 or BLIS 0.9.0), and CUDA from 11 to 12.

qa_pairs.question: Is the `StdEnv/2020` environment still supported?
qa_pairs.answer: The `StdEnv/2020` environment is deprecated and no longer supported on newer systems, though CPU-only software will still run fine.

qa_pairs.question: When did `StdEnv/2020` become the default software environment?
qa_pairs.answer: The `StdEnv/2020` environment became the default in April 2021.

qa_pairs.question: What are the default software components for `StdEnv/2020`?
qa_pairs.answer: The `StdEnv/2020` environment uses GCC 9.3.0, Intel 2020.1, and Open MPI 4.0.3 as its defaults.

qa_pairs.question: How can I activate the `StdEnv/2020` environment?
qa_pairs.answer: You can activate the `StdEnv/2020` environment using the command `module load StdEnv/2020`.

qa_pairs.question: What performance improvement was introduced in `StdEnv/2020` regarding Intel compiler binaries?
qa_pairs.answer: In `StdEnv/2020`, binaries compiled with the Intel compiler now automatically support both AVX2 and AVX512 instruction sets. These are known as multi-architecture or fat binaries.

qa_pairs.question: What is the benefit of multi-architecture binaries in `StdEnv/2020`?
qa_pairs.answer: Multi-architecture binaries mean that when running on clusters with multiple generations of processors, such as Cedar and Graham, you don't have to manually load one of the `arch` modules if you use software packages generated by the Intel compiler.

qa_pairs.question: How did `StdEnv/2020` change the installation of many software packages?
qa_pairs.answer: Many software packages previously installed with either GCC or Intel are now installed at a lower level of the software hierarchy in `StdEnv/2020`, making the same module visible regardless of which compiler is loaded. This includes bioinformatics software and R modules.

qa_pairs.question: What was the compatibility layer tool used for `StdEnv/2020`?
qa_pairs.answer: For the `StdEnv/2020` version, Gentoo Prefix was used for the compatibility layer.

qa_pairs.question: What compatibility layer tools were used for `StdEnv/2016.4` and `StdEnv/2018.3`?
qa_pairs.answer: For `StdEnv/2016.4` and `StdEnv/2018.3` versions, the Nix package manager was used for the compatibility layer.

qa_pairs.question: Is `StdEnv/2018.3` still supported?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the `StdEnv/2018.3` environment is deprecated and no longer supported.

qa_pairs.question: When was `StdEnv/2018.3` released and what major deployments was it associated with?
qa_pairs.answer: The `StdEnv/2018.3` environment was released in 2018 with the deployment of Béluga, and shortly after the deployment of Niagara.

qa_pairs.question: What were the default components for `StdEnv/2018.3`?
qa_pairs.answer: Defaults for `StdEnv/2018.3` were GCC 7.3.0, Intel 2018.3, and Open MPI 3.1.2.

qa_pairs.question: Which standard environment was the first to support AVX512 instructions?
qa_pairs.answer: `StdEnv/2018.3` was the first version to support AVX512 instructions.

qa_pairs.question: How do I activate the `StdEnv/2018.3` environment?
qa_pairs.answer: You can activate this environment using the command `module load StdEnv/2018.3`.

qa_pairs.question: Is `StdEnv/2016.4` still supported?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the `StdEnv/2016.4` environment is deprecated and no longer supported.

qa_pairs.question: When was `StdEnv/2016.4` released and with what systems?
qa_pairs.answer: The `StdEnv/2016.4` environment was the initial version released in 2016 with the deployment of Cedar and Graham.

qa_pairs.question: What were the default compilers and MPI implementation for `StdEnv/2016.4`?
qa_pairs.answer: The `StdEnv/2016.4` environment featured GCC 5.4.0 and Intel 2016.4 as default compilers, and Open MPI 2.1.1 as its default implementation of MPI.

qa_pairs.question: Does software compiled with `StdEnv/2016.4` support AVX512 instructions?
qa_pairs.answer: Most software compiled with `StdEnv/2016.4` does not support AVX512 instructions, which are provided by Skylake processors on systems like Béluga, Niagara, and newer additions to Cedar and Graham.

qa_pairs.question: What command should I use to activate `StdEnv/2016.4`?
qa_pairs.answer: To activate this environment, use the command `module load StdEnv/2016.4`.
